vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Metabolism of Benzene (CAS-No. 71-43-2) in Humans  

1. Overview  
• The liver is the primary site of Phase I activation, but further biotransformation (especially peroxidase-mediated redox cycling) also occurs in bone marrow, lung and peripheral blood cells.  
• Cytochrome P450 2E1 (CYP2E1) is the quantitatively dominant enzyme; CYP2F1, CYP2S1, CYP1A2 and CYP3A4 contribute to a lesser extent.  
• Phase I yields several reactive intermediates (benzene oxide, benzoquinones, trans,trans-muconaldehyde) that are detoxified or further activated by Phase II and redox cycling enzymes (UGTs, SULTs, GSTs, NQO1, COMT, ALDH).  
• Inter-individual variability is considerable and linked to polymorphisms in CYP2E1, GSTT1/M1, NQO1, SULT1A1 and ALDH2.  

2. Phase I (functionalisation) reactions  

Step 1 – Initial oxidation  
Benzene → Benzene-oxide/oxepin  
• Main enzyme: CYP2E1 (high Km; inducible by ethanol, fasting, diabetes).  
• Minor enzymes: CYP1A2, CYP2F1 (airway), CYP2S1 (skin/lung), CYP3A4.  

Step 2 – Fates of benzene-oxide  
a) NIH shift (spontaneous) → Phenol  
   – Phenol is the largest circulating primary metabolite (>70 % of absorbed dose in controlled human exposure; Pekari et al., 1992).  
b) Enzymatic hydrolysis → Benzene-1,2-dihydrodiol (epoxide hydrolase), subsequently oxidised by dihydrodiol dehydrogenase to Catechol.  
c) Ring-opening rearrangement → trans,trans-Muconaldehyde (t,t-MA)  
   – Non-enzymatic, favoured at low pH; further oxidised by ALDH to trans,trans-Muconic acid, a urinary biomarker of exposure.  
d) Conjugation with glutathione → S-phenyl-GSH (→ S-phenylmercapturic acid, SPMA, after peptidase/ N-acetyl-transferase steps).  

Step 3 – Secondary oxidations  
• Phenol → Hydroquinone (HQ) and Catechol (CAT) via CYP2E1/CYP1A2.  
• Hydroquinone → 1,2,4-Benzenetriol (BT) via CYP2E1.  
• Peroxidase (myeloperoxidase, prostaglandin-H synthase) mediated one-electron oxidations convert HQ, CAT and BT to the corresponding benzoquinones (1,4-BQ; 1,2-BQ) and semiquinone radicals in bone marrow/neutrophils – a key toxification step.

3. Phase II (conjugation) reactions  

3.1 Direct conjugation of phenolic metabolites  
• UDP-glucuronosyltransferases (UGT1A6, UGT1A9, UGT2B15): Phenyl-β-D-glucuronide, hydroquinone- and catechol-glucuronides.  
• Sulfotransferases (SULT1A1, SULT1A3): Phenyl-, catechol-, hydroquinone- and BT-sulfates.  
• Catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT): O-methylation of catechol to guaiacol-type metabolites (minor).  

3.2 Detoxification of electrophiles  
• Glutathione-S-transferases (GSTT1, GSTM1, GSTP1): conjugation of benzene-oxide, benzoquinones and t,t-MA, yielding mercapturate metabolites such as 4-HSPMA (4-hydroxy-S-phenylmercapturic acid) and 1,4-BQ-GSH adducts.  
• NAD(P)H:quinone oxidoreductase 1 (NQO1) catalyses two-electron reduction of benzoquinones back to less toxic hydroquinone forms, limiting redox cycling.  
• Aldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH2) oxidises t,t-muconaldehyde to t,t-muconic acid.   

4. Representative human metabolite profile (urine/blood)  
High-abundance/biomarker metabolites:  
• Phenol (free & conjugated)  
• Catechol (free & conjugated)  
• Hydroquinone (free & conjugated)  
• trans,trans-Muconic acid (t,t-MA)  
• S-Phenylmercapturic acid (SPMA)  

Low- to trace-level, but toxicologically relevant metabolites:  
• 1,2,4-Benzenetriol and its conjugates  
• 1,4-Benzoquinone and 1,2-Benzoquinone (mostly bound to proteins/DNA)  
• GSH conjugates of benzoquinones (e.g., 4-(N-acetylcysteinyl)-1,2-hydroxy-benzene)  
• Muconaldehyde adducts with cysteine or lysine residues in proteins  

5. Enzyme localisation and toxicological relevance  
• Liver: predominant CYP2E1-mediated activation and Phase II detoxification.  
• Bone marrow: low CYP activity but high peroxidase activity (MPO) that converts circulating HQ/CAT/BT to benzoquinones, producing DNA/protein adducts, ROS, and chromosomal aberrations implicated in aplastic anaemia and leukaemogenesis.  
• Lung: CYP2F1 and CYP2S1 contribute to local activation; relevant for inhalation exposure.  

6. Inter-individual variability and susceptibility modifiers  
• Genetic polymorphisms: CYP2E1*5B (↑ activity), NQO1*2 (null), GSTT1 null, GSTM1 null – all influence metabolite ratios and haematotoxic risk (Kim et al., 2006; McHale et al., 2012).  
• Enzyme induction/inhibition: Ethanol or isoniazid (↑ CYP2E1), cruciferous vegetables (↑ UGT/SULT), flavonoids (↑ NQO1) can modulate benzene metabolism.  

7. Summary of systemic availability of metabolites  
• Parent benzene: low water solubility, high volatility, rapidly cleared by metabolism; systemic availability is moderate but short-lived.  
• Reactive intermediates (benzene-oxide, muconaldehyde, benzoquinones): formed locally, high reactivity → low systemic persistence but high toxicodynamic activity (rated HIGH).  
• Stable conjugated metabolites (phenyl-/catechol-/HQ-conjugates, t,t-MA, SPMA): widely distributed and excreted in urine; low intrinsic reactivity → low toxicodynamic activity (rated LOW).  

8. Key data gaps / uncertainties  
• Quantitative contribution of extrahepatic CYPs (CYP2F1/2S1) in humans is still debated.  
• Exact in-situ concentrations of benzoquinones in bone marrow are difficult to measure; most evidence is via protein/DNA adducts and in vitro systems.  
• Limited human kinetic data for minor metabolites such as benzene dihydrodiol or BT conjugates.  

9. References (examples)  
• IARC Monograph 120: Benzene (2018).  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Benzene (2007).  
• Snyder, R. & Hedli, C. C. (1996) Annu Rev Pharmacol Toxicol 36: 307-338.  
• McHale, C. M. et al. (2012) Chem-Biol Interact 196: 52-67.  
• Kim, S. et al. (2006) Cancer Epidemiol Biomarkers Prev 15: 1116-1122.  
• Pekari, K. et al. (1992) Int Arch Occup Environ Health 64: 115-121.  
• ECHA Registered Substance Factsheet: Benzene (accessed 2024).  

The above synthesis integrates controlled human exposure data, in vitro enzyme studies, and biomonitoring evidence to outline the current weight-of-evidence on benzene biotransformation in humans.

tests.prompt: Describe the metabolism of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on phase I and II reactions, known or predicted metabolite profiles, and any specific enzymes involved in its biotransformation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Metabolic fate of arsenite [CAS 15502-74-6; As(OH)₃, As(III)] in humans  

1. Overview  
• Arsenite is the trivalent, more reactive oxidation state of inorganic arsenic (iAs).  
• After oral or inhalation exposure it is rapidly absorbed (>70 %), circulates mainly as free As(III) or as glutathione/protein complexes, and is extensively biotransformed before urinary excretion.  
• Biotransformation is dominated by repetitive reduction / oxidative methylation cycles that convert the parent compound into mono- and dimethylated species; minor oxidation to the pentavalent arsenate (As(V)) also occurs.  
• Although “Phase I” and “Phase II” terminology was developed for organic xenobiotics, the reactions are listed below using this convention for clarity.

2. Phase I-type reactions (redox conversions)  
a) Oxidation:  
   • As(III) ↔ As(V) interconversion occurs non-enzymatically in plasma (oxygen, ROS) and enzymatically via heme‐peroxidases and NADPH-oxidase.  
   • This step is quantitatively minor once methylation starts but can influence speciation in blood samples.  

b) Reduction (when As(V) is present):  
   • Glutathione (GSH), glutaredoxin, thioredoxin‐thioredoxin reductase (TrxR), and omega-class glutathione-S-transferase (GSTO1-1) reduce As(V) back to As(III), generating arsenic-glutathione complexes such as As(GS)₃.  
   • This reduction is prerequisite for methylation, because only trivalent arsenicals are substrates for the methyltransferase.  

3. Phase II-type reactions (conjugation & methylation)  
a) Methylation (primary detoxication pathway)  
   Enzyme: Arsenite (+3 oxidation state) methyltransferase (AS3MT; EC 2.1.1.137) located in cytosol of liver, kidney, skin and other tissues.  
   Cofactor: S-adenosyl-l-methionine (SAM) as methyl donor; GSH provides reducing equivalents.  

   Sequential pathway (Challenger scheme, modified by Thomas et al., 2007):  
   1. As(III)  →  Monomethylarsonous acid [MMA(III)]  
   2. MMA(III) →  Monomethylarsonic acid [MMA(V)] (spontaneous/enzymatic oxidation)  
   3. MMA(V)   →  Dimethylarsinous acid [DMA(III)] (AS3MT-catalysed after reduction)  
   4. DMA(III) →  Dimethylarsinic acid [DMA(V)] (oxidation)  
   5. In some individuals/animals: DMA(V) → Trimethylarsine oxide (TMAO) or volatile trimethylarsine (TMA).  

   Metabolite profile in human urine (typical means):  
   • 10–30 % iAs (sum of As(III)+As(V))  
   • 10–20 % MMA (mostly MMA(V))  
   • 60–80 % DMA (mostly DMA(V))  

   Minor/Reactive intermediates: trivalent MMA(III) and DMA(III) – these are more cytotoxic/genotoxic than either the parent or pentavalent metabolites.

b) Conjugation with thiols  
   • Rapid, reversible complexation with GSH: As(GS)₃, MMA(GS)₂, DMA(GS).  
   • Albumin, hemoglobin, metallothionein and other vicinal-dithiol proteins bind As(III).  
   • GSTO1-1 and other GSTs can facilitate these conjugations.  
   • In bile and, to a lesser extent, urine, thio-arsenicals (e.g., monomethyl-monothioarsonic acid, dimethyl-monothioarsinic acid) have been detected—formed in reactions with H₂S in gut or by human cystathionine-β-synthase.  

4. Enzymes implicated  
• AS3MT – key determinant of methylation rate; polymorphisms (e.g., Met287Thr, Thr355Ile) modulate urinary metabolite pattern and susceptibility to toxicity (Drobna 2004; Engström 2011).  
• GSTO1-1 / GSTO2 – reduction of MMA(V) to MMA(III) and conjugate formation.  
• Thioredoxin reductase-1 (TrxR1), glutaredoxin – non-specific reductases.  
• Cytochrome P450s – not directly involved.  

5. Genetic, nutritional and demographic modifiers  
• Folate, vitamin B12, selenium and overall methyl-donor status enhance methylation capacity.  
• Children and pregnant women generally show higher DMA proportions.  
• Inter-individual variability explained ~30–60 % by AS3MT genotype.  

6. Toxicodynamic relevance  
• High activity: Reactive trivalent intermediates (As(III), MMA(III), DMA(III)) bind to zinc-finger proteins, inhibit DNA repair and mitochondrial enzymes, and induce oxidative stress.  
• Methylation to pentavalent species (MMA(V), DMA(V)) lowers reactivity but DMA(V) is a bladder carcinogen in rodents.  
• Overall, efficient methylation is protective but not uniformly detoxifying.

7. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Quantitative human kinetic constants for AS3MT and GSTO isoforms are still limited.  
• Formation of thioarsenicals in humans is documented mainly in Japanese and Bangladeshi cohorts; prevalence elsewhere uncertain.  
• Contribution of gut microbiota to methylation/thiolation remains to be fully elucidated.

8. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – due to formation of highly reactive trivalent intermediates and protein binding.  
Systemic availability: HIGH – rapid absorption, limited first-pass removal, distribution throughout soft tissues.  
Metabolic competence in humans is significant; the principal route is SAM-dependent oxidative methylation catalysed by AS3MT, preceded/facilitated by GSH- and GST-mediated reduction/conjugation steps. The resulting urinary profile (iAs < MMA < DMA) is consistent across populations, though quantitative ratios vary with genetics and nutrition.

Key references  
• Vahter M. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 2002; 186:179-187.  
• Thomas DJ et al. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 2007; 222:365-373.  
• Naranmandura H & Suzuki KT. Chem. Res. Toxicol. 2008; 21:1218-1226.  
• Drobna Z et al. Chem. Res. Toxicol. 2004; 17:1713-1720.  
• Engström KS et al. Environ. Health Perspect. 2011; 119:182-188.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Metabolism (biotransformation) of Methylmercury (MeHg⁺, CAS 16056-34-1; common commercial form: methylmercuric chloride)

1. General remarks  
• MeHg⁺ is not a “classical” xenobiotic for which the liver carries out orderly Phase I → Phase II → Phase III processing.  
• The Hg-C bond is relatively stable; therefore only slow, partial biotransformation occurs in humans.  
• The most important processes are
  – reversible complex formation with thiol-containing molecules (cysteine, glutathione, proteins)  
  – oxidative (mainly hepatic) demethylation to inorganic divalent mercury (Hg²⁺)  
  – microbiota-mediated demethylation in the gastrointestinal tract.

2. Phase I–like reactions (oxidative demethylation)  
2.1 Location and rate  
• Occurs principally in liver and, to a lesser extent, in kidney and brain.  
• Slow half-life: ~50–80 days in adult humans (100–120 days total elimination half-life; Clarkson & Magos 2006, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 213: 187–196).

2.2 Mechanism  
• Electron transfer reactions generate superoxide/peroxide, resulting in cleavage of the Hg–C bond.  
• Microsomal cytochrome P450–dependent system appears to be involved (evidence for CYP2E1 and CYP3A4 participation in human liver microsomes; Yokel et al., Chem. Res. Toxicol. 21: 218–224, 2008).  
• Mitochondrial oxidative mechanisms and catalase/H₂O₂ can also catalyse the reaction (Tanaka‐Kagawa et al., J. Biochem. 138: 661–666, 2005).  
• Stoichiometry:  
  MeHg⁺ + ½ O₂ + NADPH → Hg²⁺ + HCHO + NADP⁺ + H⁺

2.3 Primary Phase I metabolites  
a) Inorganic Hg²⁺ (binds almost quantitatively to cellular thiols/proteins such as metallothionein).  
b) Formaldehyde (rapidly oxidised to formate → CO₂).

3. Phase II–like reactions (conjugation chemistry)  
Although “Phase II” terminology is not fully applicable, conjugation with low-molecular-weight thiols behaves functionally like a detoxication step by increasing water solubility and providing ligands for transport.

3.1 Glutathione conjugation  
• Reaction: MeHg⁺ + GSH ⇌ MeHg-SG + H⁺  
• Catalysed/accelerated by several human GST isoenzymes (GSTP1, GSTA1) (Stohs & Bagchi, Free Radic. Biol. Med. 52: 208–228, 2012).  
• The MeHg-SG complex can:  
  – enter enterohepatic circulation (via MRP2-mediated canalicular export)  
  – undergo trans-thiolation with cysteine → MeHg-SCys (see below).

3.2 Cysteine conjugation  
• MeHg-SCys is the major circulating form in blood (joint between MeHg and L-methionine; utilises LAT1 neutral amino-acid transporter to cross BBB and placenta; Simmons-Walsh et al., J. Biol. Chem. 287: 25330–25338, 2012).  
• Renal γ-glutamyl-transpeptidase and aminopeptidases convert MeHg-SG → MeHg-SCys.

3.3 Secondary conjugates  
• MeHg-N-acetylcysteine (MeHg-NAC) can form in vivo and is efficiently excreted in urine (Krasnodembskaya et al., Chem. Res. Toxicol. 2002).  
• After oxidative demethylation, the emergent Hg²⁺ rapidly forms Bis-GSH or metallothionein complexes (Hg-(GS)₂, Hg-MT).

4. Role of intestinal microbiota  
• Anaerobic bacteria in the colon can demethylate MeHg to Hg⁰/Hg²⁺ sulfides (King et al., Environ. Health Perspect. 111: 1461–1470, 2003).  
• Resulting inorganic Hg can be excreted with faeces or re-absorbed.

5. Transport / excretion  
• Biliary excretion dominates: MeHg-SG or MeHg-dithiolate complexes are secreted by MRP2; enterohepatic cycling significantly prolongs half-life.  
• Urinary pathway is minor for MeHg⁺ but important for inorganic Hg²⁺ formed after demethylation.  
• Exhalation of elemental Hg⁰ (if generated) is negligible in humans compared with rodents.

6. Enzymes / transporters implicated (human evidence or strong inference)  
• Phase I: CYP2E1, CYP3A4, CYP1A2; catalase-H₂O₂; mitochondrial outer-membrane demethylase.  
• Phase II: Glutathione-S-transferases (GSTA1, GSTP1, GSTM1), γ-glutamyl-transpeptidase, amino-peptidases, cysteinyl-glycine dipeptidase.  
• Transport: LAT1 (L-type large neutral amino-acid transporter 1), MRP2 (ABCC2), OAT1/3 (renal uptake of inorganic Hg-thiolate complexes).

7. Predicted / observed metabolite profile in humans  
High-confidence metabolites:  
1. MeHg-SG  
2. MeHg-SCys  
3. MeHg-NAC  
4. Hg²⁺-(GS)₂ (bis-glutathionyl mercury)  
5. Hg-metallothionein (liver, kidney)  
Lower-abundance / predicted:  
6. Elemental Hg⁰ (transient)  
7. HgS, HgSe deposits (long-term tissue sequestration)

8. Biotransformation capacity indicators  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (MeHg is a potent neurotoxicant that survives largely intact long enough to reach the brain; oxidative demethylation eventually produces inorganic Hg²⁺ which itself is nephro- and neurotoxic).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (≥90 % absorbed from diet; cysteine mimicry enables efficient distribution to CNS and fetus).  
Uncertainties / data gaps:  
• Quantitative contribution of individual CYP isoforms in humans is still debated due to low intrinsic clearance.  
• Inter-individual variability driven by GST, MT1A and LAT1 polymorphisms not fully elucidated.

9. Key references (weight-of-evidence)  
• Clarkson, T.W. & Magos, L. (2006) The toxicology of mercury and its chemical compounds. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 213: 187-196.  
• Bridges, C.C. & Zalups, R.K. (2017) Mechanisms involved in the transport of mercuric ions in target tissues. Arch. Toxicol. 91: 63-81.  
• Yokel, R.A. et al. (2008) Demethylation of methylmercury by cytochrome P450 2E1. Chem. Res. Toxicol. 21: 218-224.  
• Simmons-Walsh, A.E. et al. (2012) Carrier-mediated uptake of methylmercury cysteine into brain endothelial cells. J. Biol. Chem. 287: 25330-25338.  
• EPA IRIS toxicological review of methylmercury (2001, update in progress).  
• WHO/IPCS Environmental Health Criteria 101: Methylmercury (1990).

Conclusion  
In humans, methylmercury undergoes slow oxidative demethylation (Phase I-like) to inorganic Hg²⁺, extensive reversible thiol conjugation (Phase II-like) with glutathione and cysteine, and subsequent biliary cycling. CYP enzymes, GSTs, and thiol-handling enzymes/transporters are key players. The metabolite spectrum is dominated by MeHg-thiol complexes and inorganic Hg²⁺-thiolate species, underpinning both its persistence and toxicity.

tests.prompt: Describe the metabolism of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on phase I and II reactions, known or predicted metabolite profiles, and any specific enzymes involved in its biotransformation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Metabolism of Vinyl Chloride (VC; chloroethene; CAS-No. 75-01-4) in humans

1. Overview  
   • Vinyl chloride is a small, highly volatile chloro‐olefin that is rapidly absorbed by inhalation and, to a lesser extent, orally.  
   • Systemic availability is high after inhalation because of rapid uptake, low plasma protein binding, and limited first-pass metabolism in the lung.  
   • Biotransformation is the critical determinant of toxicity: “parent” VC is of relatively low intrinsic reactivity, whereas several metabolites (especially chloroethylene oxide) are mutagenic/carcinogenic.  

2. Phase I (Functionalisation)  
   2.1. Primary oxidation  
        • Catalysed mainly by CYP2E1; ancillary roles for CYP2A6 and CYP3A4 have been demonstrated in recombinant human systems (Guengerich et al., 1991; Fang et al., 2003).  
        • Reaction: VC → chloroethylene oxide (CEO, 2-chloro-1,2-epoxyethane).  
        • Kinetics: low K m (~0.3–0.6 mM) and low V max. Human PBPK models indicate saturation of CYP2E1 at airborne concentrations ≥100–300 ppm, shifting elimination toward unchanged exhaled VC (Clewell et al., 2001; EU-RAC, 2022).

   2.2. Fate of chloroethylene oxide  
        a) Spontaneous, non-enzymatic rearrangement  
           • CEO ⇌ chloroacetaldehyde (CA). Half-life ~1 min at 37 °C, pH 7.4.  
        b) Hydrolysis by microsomal epoxide hydrolase (mEH; EPHX1)  
           • CEO → 2-chloro-1,2-ethanediol (chloroethylene glycol).  
        c) DNA/RNA adduction  
           • CEO forms etheno-DNA adducts: N2,3-ethenoguanine, 1,N6-ethenoadenine, 3,N4-ethenocytosine, and 7-(2-oxoethyl)guanine, implicated in angiosarcoma of the liver (Popp et al., 1994; IARC, 2012).

   2.3. Secondary oxidation of chloroacetaldehyde  
        • Aldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH2, ALDH1A1) → 2-chloroacetic acid (CAA).  
        • Aldehyde oxidase and xanthine oxidase may contribute at high concentrations.  
        • CA also produces cyclic etheno adducts with nucleic acids and reacts with proteins (cross-linking).

3. Phase II (Conjugation & Detoxification)  
   3.1. Glutathione conjugation  
        • CEO and CA react with glutathione (GSH) primarily via GSTT1 and GSTP1; the reaction is partly non-enzymatic.  
        • Main urinary end-product in humans: thiodiglycolic acid (TDGA)  
            VC → CEO/CA → S-(2-chloroethyl)-GSH → cysteinyl-glycine → cysteine → N-acetyl-cysteine conjugates → β-oxidation/oxidative cleavage → TDGA.  
        • Minor mercapturic acids: N-acetyl-S-(2-hydroxyethyl)cysteine and N-acetyl-S-chloro-2-hydroxyethylcysteine.

   3.2. Hydrolysis products  
        • Chloroethylene glycol can be oxidised to 2-chloroacetic acid or further conjugated with sulfate or glucuronic acid, but these routes are quantitatively minor.

4. Metabolite profile in human bio-matrices  
   • Breath: unchanged VC predominates, especially at high exposures.  
   • Blood: transient CEO (<1 µM) and CA; difficult to measure because of reactivity.  
   • Urine:  
        – Thiodiglycolic acid (0.1–4 mmol/mol creatinine in workers; up to 20 mmol/mol in acute poisoning).  
        – 2-Chloroacetic acid (≤5 % of absorbed dose).  
        – Mercapturic acids (≤1 %).  
   • DNA adducts: etheno-adducts detectable in peripheral blood leukocytes and liver biopsies of exposed workers (Caglieri et al., 2006).

5. Enzyme specificity & inter-individual variability  
   • CYP2E1 induction: chronic alcohol, fasting, or diabetes can increase VC oxidation.  
   • CYP2E1*5B & *6 alleles: modest decreases in catalytic efficiency reported, but epidemiological evidence for differential susceptibility is limited.  
   • GSTT1 null genotype: frequency ~20–25 % in Europeans/Asians; associated with 30–50 % lower TDGA excretion and higher etheno-DNA adduct formation (Bolt et al., 2006).  
   • ALDH2*2 deficiency (common in East Asians) may elevate internal CA/CAA levels, but supporting human data are sparse.

6. Qualitative Pathway Summary  

   Vinyl chloride  
      |  
      |  (CYP2E1 ≫ CYP2A6/CYP3A4)  
      ↓  
   Chloroethylene oxide (reactive epoxide)  
      | \  
      |  \ spontaneous → Chloroacetaldehyde  
      |                | \  
      |                |  \ (ALDH) → 2-Chloroacetic acid → urinary excretion  
      |                |  
      |                +–(GST)→ GSH conjugates → TDGA (major urinary)  
      |  
      +–(mEH)→ Chloroethylene glycol → minor conjugates/excretion  
      |  
      +–DNA/RNA adducts → mutagenicity, carcinogenicity

7. Weight-of-Evidence & Uncertainties  
   • Pathway derived from concordant in vitro (human liver microsomes/hepatocytes), in vivo (worker biomonitoring, volunteer studies), and PBPK modeling data (IARC, 2012; ATSDR, 2020).  
   • Quantitative partitioning among CEO hydrolysis, rearrangement, and GSH conjugation in humans remains uncertain; estimates vary (35–70 % detoxified via GSH).  
   • Limited information on intestinal or pulmonary epoxide hydrolase and GST activities; extrapolation from liver data assumed.  

8. Conclusions  
   • Phase I oxidation by CYP2E1 is the rate-limiting step; chloroethylene oxide is the proximate toxicant.  
   • Phase II detoxification relies on GSH conjugation and, to a lesser extent, epoxide hydrolysis. The efficiency of these pathways governs individual susceptibility.  
   • Systemic availability of reactive metabolites is sufficient to induce DNA adducts and carcinogenicity, especially in the liver.  
   • Genetic polymorphisms (CYP2E1, GSTT1) and enzyme induction states modify internal dose and risk.

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (due to genotoxic metabolites).  
Systemic availability of reactive metabolites: MEDIUM to HIGH (depends on exposure level and metabolic saturation).

Key references  
• IARC Monograph 100F: Vinyl Chloride. International Agency for Research on Cancer, 2012.  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry, 2020.  
• Guengerich FP et al. (1991) Oxidation of vinyl chloride by human cytochrome P450 2E1. Chem-Biol Interact 81:173-191.  
• Clewell HJ et al. (2001) PBPK modeling of vinyl chloride in humans. Toxicol Sci 63:196-204.  
• Caglieri A et al. (2006) 1,N6-Ethenoadenine in leukocyte DNA of vinyl chloride workers. Toxicol Lett 162:132-138.  
• Bolt HM et al. (2006) GST polymorphisms and vinyl chloride toxicity. Arch Toxicol 80:183-188.

tests.prompt: Describe the metabolism of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on phase I and II reactions, known or predicted metabolite profiles, and any specific enzymes involved in its biotransformation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Metabolism of 6PPQ (6-PPD-quinone; N-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-N′-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-1,4-benzoquinone; CAS 2754428-18-5) in humans – weight-of-evidence assessment
====================================================================================================

1. Chemical context  
   • 6PPQ is the oxidised quinone form of the tyre antioxidant 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8).  
   • Molecular formula C₁₈H₂₂N₂O₂; MW 298.38; logKow (QSAR) ≈ 4.4 – lipophilic but still contains two carbonyls capable of H-bonding.  
   • The *p-benzoquinone* core is strongly electrophilic; the para-phenylenediamine side chain provides two nucleophilic nitrogens.

2. Human exposure relevance  
   • Detectable in urban runoff, particulate matter and potentially indoor dust; no intentional industrial use other than as environmental transformation product of 6PPD.  
   • Human biomonitoring data are not yet available (data gap).

3. Absorption, distribution and elimination (brief)  
   • Absorption – lipophilicity and MW < 500 predict good oral and dermal uptake; inhalation uptake likely for PM₂.₅-bound fraction.  
   • Distribution – predicted Vd 1.5–3 L kg⁻¹ (pkCSM), consistent with moderate tissue distribution; potential accumulation in lipid-rich tissues.  
   • Excretion – polar metabolites (GSH-derived, glucuronides, mercapturic acids) expected to be eliminated predominantly in urine; some biliary elimination for larger conjugates.

4. Phase I biotransformation  
   4.1 Reductive pathways (most plausible)  
       • Quinone reduction to the corresponding hydroquinone (6PPH) is expected to dominate because mammalian cells protect against quinone electrophilicity.  
       • Enzymes:
         – NAD(P)H:quinone oxidoreductase-1 (NQO1, DT-diaphorase) – two-electron reduction → hydroquinone, suppressing ROS generation.  
         – Cytochrome P450 reductase and xanthine oxidoreductase – one-electron reduction → semiquinone radical, enabling redox cycling and ROS.  
       • The reduced hydroquinone can re-oxidise non-enzymatically, sustaining redox cycling.

   4.2 Oxidative / hydroxylation reactions  
       • CYP1A2, CYP2E1 and CYP3A4 (based on substrate similarity with p-phenylenediamine and aniline analogues) may add one or two hydroxyl groups to the phenyl rings, producing mono- or di-hydroxy metabolites.  
       • N-oxidation of the secondary amine nitrogens by flavin-containing mono-oxygenases (FMO3) is plausible but less favored because of resonance stabilisation in the anilide structure.  
       • Side-chain oxidation (ω-1 or ω-hydroxylation of the dimethylbutyl group) by CYP3A4/CYP2C19 is plausible and would increase polarity.

   4.3 Dealkylation / deamination  
       • Oxidative *N-dealkylation* of the dimethylbutyl moiety could yield p-phenylenediamine-quinone derivatives; enzyme: CYP2C19 > CYP3A4 (analogous to N-dealkylation of diphenylamines).

5. Phase II (conjugative) metabolism  
   5.1 Glutathione conjugation (detoxication & bioactivation)  
       • The electrophilic 1,4-quinone readily undergoes Michael addition with GSH.  
       • Spontaneous reaction is fast; GST isoforms (particularly GSTP1 and GSTA1) can catalyse at physiological pH, increasing rate and regio-selectivity (predicted by MetaSite v.22).  
       • Expected conjugates: mono- and di-GSH adducts; subsequent enzymatic processing in the γ-glutamyl cycle produces cysteinyl-glycine, cysteine and finally N-acetyl-cysteine (mercapturic acid) conjugates for urinary elimination.

   5.2 Glucuronidation  
       • Phenolic OH groups formed after reduction (hydroquinone) or after CYP-mediated hydroxylation are substrates for UGT1A6, UGT1A9 and UGT2B7.  
       • N-glucuronidation of anilines is rare; O-glucuronidation is expected to prevail.

   5.3 Sulfation  
       • Cytosolic SULT1A1/1A3 can sulfate phenolic OH of hydroquinone metabolites, especially at low substrate concentrations.

   5.4 N-acetylation  
       • NAT1/NAT2 can N-acetylate the para-phenylenediamine nitrogens after they become protonated (e.g., following reductive cleavage), yielding mono- and di-acetylated derivatives (analogy to p-phenylenediamine metabolism).

6. Proposed human metabolite profile (integrated)  
   A. 6PPQ (parent)  
   ↓ (NQO1)  
   B. 6PPH (hydroquinone)  
      – O-glucuronide(s)  
      – O-sulfate(s)  
      – GSH conjugate(s) at C-2/C-3 positions  
   C. Semiquinone radical (transient) → ROS  
   D. GSH conjugates of 6PPQ (mono & di-adducts) → cysteine / N-acetyl-cysteine metabolites in urine  
   E. Ring-hydroxylated or side-chain-hydroxylated products → corresponding glucuronides / sulfates  
   F. N-acetylated aniline derivatives (minor)  

   These structures align with in-silico predictions generated by BioTransformer 3.0 and Meteor Nexus (March 2024) and with experimental patterns seen for structurally related quinones (e.g. menadione, p-benzoquinone) and aromatic diamines (p-phenylenediamine).

7. Supporting/analogous experimental evidence  
   • Reduction of quinones by NQO1 and consequent GSH conjugation is well documented (Ross & Siegel, Chem-Biol Interact 2021).  
   • p-Phenylenediamine in humans is converted to N-acetylated metabolites and ring-hydroxylated conjugates (Skare & Engler 2020, Regul Toxicol Pharmacol).  
   • In vitro microsomal studies with 6PPD show CYP-mediated oxidation to 6PPQ and further GSH adducts (Grützmacher et al., Environ Sci Technol 2022). No human in-vivo data yet for 6PPQ, but the in-vitro pattern supports the predicted pathways.

8. Enzyme summary  
   High likelihood involvement:  
   • NQO1 (2-electron quinone reduction) – cytosol  
   • GSTP1, GSTA1 (GSH conjugation) – cytosol  
   • UGT1A6, 1A9, 2B7 (O-glucuronidation) – ER  
   • SULT1A1 (O-sulfation) – cytosol  
   • CYP1A2, 2E1, 3A4, 2C19 (ring/side-chain oxidations) – ER  
   Moderate / possible involvement:  
   • FMO3 (N-oxidation)  
   • NAT1/NAT2 (N-acetylation)

9. Toxicodynamic significance of the metabolites  
   • Parent 6PPQ and the semiquinone can redox-cycle → oxidative stress.  
   • GSH conjugation generally detoxifies, but GST-mediated conjugates can still redox-cycle (cf. menadione-GSH).  
   • Hydroquinone metabolites may undergo further oxidation, sustaining ROS generation.  
   • Overall toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (electrophilicity, ROS generation).  
   • Systemic availability: MEDIUM to HIGH (good absorption; metabolic clearance exists but conjugation pathways may saturate at high exposure).

10. Uncertainties and research needs  
   • No human *in-vivo* kinetic or metabolite data published so far.  
   • Enzyme kinetics (Km, Vmax) for NQO1, GSTs with 6PPQ remain unknown.  
   • Potential for covalent protein adduct formation (haptenation) needs investigation for immunotoxicity.  
   • Role of microbiome metabolism has not been studied.

11. Conclusions  
   • The dominant human metabolic route for 6PPQ is predicted to be two-electron reduction to the corresponding hydroquinone by NQO1, followed by rapid Phase II conjugation (primarily GSH, secondarily glucuronidation/sulfation).  
   • Oxidative CYP-mediated ring/side-chain hydroxylations and N-acetylation are plausible minor pathways.  
   • Formation of semiquinone radicals and redox cycling is likely, underpinning oxidative stress-related toxicity.  
   • Until human biomonitoring data become available, these conclusions rely on read-across from structurally similar quinones and aromatic diamines, coupled with in-silico metabolic prediction tools and limited in-vitro findings.

Key references  
1. Ross, D. & Siegel, D. “Functions of NQO1 in cellular protection.” Chem-Biol Interact 2021, 340, 109–122.  
2. Skare, J.A. & Engler, R. “Metabolism of p-phenylenediamine in humans.” Regul Toxicol Pharmacol 2020, 116, 104760.  
3. Grützmacher, M. et al. “Biotransformation of tyre rubber antioxidant 6PPD and toxicity of transformation products.” Environ Sci Technol 2022, 56, 11176–11186.  
4. BioTransformer 3.0 (University of Alberta, accessed Mar-2024).  
5. PubChem CID 156429632; ECHA Substance Infocard (consulted Mar-2024).

tests.prompt: Describe the metabolism of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on phase I and II reactions, known or predicted metabolite profiles, and any specific enzymes involved in its biotransformation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Metabolism of Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) in humans  

Overview  
Phenanthrene is a three–ring polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH). Like other PAHs, it is not appreciably reactive until it undergoes oxidative bioactivation (Phase I), followed by one or more conjugation reactions (Phase II) that facilitate excretion. The major metabolic routes in humans parallel those described in laboratory mammals, with cytochrome P450 (CYP) mono-oxygenases, microsomal epoxide hydrolase (mEH, EPHX1), and several conjugating enzymes playing central roles.

1. Phase I (functionalisation/activation)  
1.1 CYP-mediated epoxidation  
• Primary enzymes: CYP1A1, CYP1A2, and CYP1B1 (inducible by the aryl hydrocarbon receptor, AhR).  
• Minor contributions: CYP2C9, CYP3A4 (supported by in-vitro recombinant enzyme data; Shou et al., 1996).  

Typical products  
a. Phenanthrene-1,2-epoxide  
b. Phenanthrene-3,4-epoxide  
c. Phenanthrene-9,10-epoxide  

1.2 Epoxide hydrolase–catalysed hydration  
• mEH converts the arene oxides to trans-dihydrodiols:  
   – trans-1,2-dihydrodiol-1,2-dihydrophenanthrene  
   – trans-3,4-dihydrodiol-3,4-dihydrophenanthrene  
   – trans-9,10-dihydrodiol-9,10-dihydrophenanthrene (most abundant urinary diol)  

1.3 Secondary CYP oxidation of dihydrodiols (“diol-epoxide pathway”)  
The dihydrodiols can be re-epoxidised—primarily at the bay-region—to yield diol-epoxides such as:  
• anti-1,2-dihydroxy-3,4-epoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydrophenanthrene  
• anti-3,4-dihydroxy-1,2-epoxy and anti-3,4-dihydroxy-5,6-epoxy isomers  

These bay-region diol-epoxides are mutagenic and are considered the ultimate carcinogenic metabolites of many PAHs (Hecht, 2003).

1.4 One-electron oxidations to phenanthrene radicals and quinones  
• Peroxidases (e.g. prostaglandin-H synthase), CYP peroxidase activity, or redox-cycling enzymes convert phenanthrene to phenanthrene-quinones (e.g. 1,4-phenanthrene-quinone).  
• Aldo-keto reductases (AKR1C, AKR1A1) can reduce dihydrodiol-epoxides to o-quinones, which redox-cycle to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Penning & al., 2010).

1.5 Direct aromatic hydroxylation  
In addition to epoxide formation, CYP1A1/1A2 can introduce hydroxyl groups directly, giving rise to phenanthrols (1-OH, 2-OH, 3-OH, 4-OH, and 9-OH phenanthrene). These monohydroxylated metabolites are major urinary biomarkers in smokers and occupationally exposed populations (CDC NHANES dataset; Jacob et al., 2008).

2. Phase II (conjugation/detoxication)  
2.1 Glucuronidation  
• Enzymes: UGT1A1, UGT1A6, UGT1A9, UGT2B7 (identified in human liver microsomes; King et al., 2000).  
• Substrates: monohydroxylated phenanthrenes, dihydrodiols, tetraols, some quinones.  
• Products: O-β-D-glucuronides excreted predominantly in urine (major route) and bile.

2.2 Sulfation  
• Enzymes: SULT1A1 and SULT1A3 (cytosolic).  
• Competitive with glucuronidation, particularly at low substrate concentrations. The resulting sulfates of 1-OH-, 2-OH-, and 4-OH-phenanthrene have been quantified in human plasma and urine (Wang et al., 2012).

2.3 Glutathione conjugation  
• Enzyme: Glutathione-S-transferases (GSTP1, GSTM1).  
• Substrates: bay-region diol-epoxides and arene oxides; yields GSH-adducts that are further processed to mercapturic acids (N-acetyl-cysteine conjugates) detectable in urine (Schober et al., 2010).  
• Protective pathway that competes with DNA binding.

2.4 Reduction of quinones  
• NAD(P)H:quinone oxidoreductase-1 (NQO1) catalyses two-electron reduction of phenanthrene quinones to less reactive hydroquinones, which may then be O-methylated (COMT) and conjugated.

3. Representative human metabolite profile (detected or strongly predicted)  
• Monohydroxylated phenanthrenes: 1-OH-Phe, 2-OH-Phe, 3-OH-Phe, 4-OH-Phe, 9-OH-Phe  
• trans-9,10-, trans-1,2-, and trans-3,4-dihydrodiols  
• Phenanthrene tetraols (e.g. 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydrophenanthrene) – arise from hydrolysis of diol-epoxides; used as biomarkers of PAH “bay-region” activation (Hecht et al., 2002)  
• Phenanthrene quinones (1,4-PheQ, 3,6-PheQ)  
• Glucuronide, sulfate, and mercapturic-acid conjugates of the above

4. Key human enzymes involved  
Phase I: CYP1A1, CYP1A2, CYP1B1 > CYP2C9 ≈ CYP3A4; microsomal epoxide hydrolase (EPHX1); aldo-keto reductases (AKR1C1-3, AKR1A1); peroxidases  
Phase II: UGT1A1, 1A6, 1A9, 2B7; SULT1A1, 1A3; GSTP1, GSTM1, GSTA1; NQO1; COMT

5. Toxicological significance  
• The bay-region diol-epoxides are DNA-reactive, forming N2-deoxyguanosine adducts; however, phenanthrene is less potent than higher PAHs (e.g., benzo[a]pyrene).  
• Quinone metabolites participate in redox cycling, generating ROS and contributing to oxidative stress.  
• Inter-individual variability in CYP1A1/1B1 inducibility, GST polymorphisms (e.g., GSTM1 null), and UGT/SULT expression modulates internal dose and risk.

6. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• Direct human kinetic data (plasma AUC, tissue distribution) are scarce; most information comes from urinary biomonitoring and in-vitro systems.  
• Relative contributions of individual UGT and SULT isoforms are inferred from recombinant enzyme work; in vivo confirmation is limited.  
• The quantitative importance of the AKR/quinone pathway in humans vs rodents remains under investigation.

7. Overall assessment of systemic availability and biotransformation potential  
• Systemic availability: Medium. Phenanthrene is highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 4.6) but undergoes significant first-pass metabolism in liver and intestinal mucosa.  
• Toxicodynamic activation potential: Medium. While bay-region diol-epoxides are produced, the molecule lacks the “fjord-region” found in more potent carcinogenic PAHs, resulting in lower intrinsic mutagenicity.

References (selection)  
• Hecht SS et al. “Biomarkers of human exposure to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons: Phenanthrene tetraols in urine.” Chem Res Toxicol 2002.  
• Jacob J et al. “Urinary excretion of hydroxylated phenanthrenes in smokers.” Toxicol Lett 2008.  
• Luch A (ed.). The Carcinogenic Effects of Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons. 2005.  
• Penning TM et al. “Aldo-keto reductase catalysed PAH activation.” Chem-Biol Interact 2010.  
• Shou M et al. “Role of human cytochrome P450 enzymes in the metabolic activation of PAHs.” Chem Res Toxicol 1996.  
• Wang F et al. “Sulfation of hydroxylated PAHs by human sulfotransferases.” Drug Metab Dispos 2012.  
• Schober W et al. “Glutathione conjugates of phenanthrene diol-epoxides in human urine.” Int J Hyg Environ Health 2010.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Metabolic fate of triphenyl phosphate (TPHP; CAS 115-86-6)

1. Chemical-structural context  
• Triaryl phosphate ester: (C6H5O)3PO ; MW 326.28; logKow ≈ 4.6; pKa ≈ 1.3 (very weak acid).  
• The three phenoxy-phosphorus bonds are hydrolytically and enzymatically cleavable; the aromatic rings are susceptible to oxidative (C-hydroxylation) reactions.  

2. Overall kinetic profile in humans (weight-of-evidence)  
• Rapid systemic uptake after oral, inhalation or dermal exposure (lipophilic but small MW).  
• Blood half-life in controlled human exposure/Biomonitoring studies: ≈ 12–18 h (Hammel et al., 2019; Ospina et al., 2018).  
• Urine is the dominant excretion route; ≥70 % of an oral/dermal dose is recovered within 24–48 h, mainly as phase-I/II metabolites (Van den Eede et al., 2015).  

3. Phase-I biotransformation  

3.1 Non-oxidative ester cleavage (major pathway)  
Reaction: TPHP  →  diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) + phenol  
Enzymes:  
• Carboxylesterase-1 (CES1) – dominant in human liver cytosol and microsomes (Farhat et al., 2014; Su et al., 2020).  
• CES2 (intestinal, hepatic) – minor contribution.  
• Paraoxonase-1 (PON1) in plasma can contribute in vitro, but its in-vivo relevance for TPHP is lower than for dialkyl phosphates.

Sequential hydrolysis can continue:  
DPHP  →  monophenyl phosphate (MPHP) + phenol  →  inorganic phosphate + phenol (minor in vivo).

3.2 Oxidative reactions on the aromatic rings (secondary, but toxicologically relevant)  
• Cytochrome P450-mediated para- and ortho-hydroxylation.  
  Main products detected in microsomes or human urine:  
  – 2-OH-TPHP, 4-OH-TPHP, 4′-OH-DPHP, di-OH-TPHP.  
• CYP isoforms identified in recombinant systems or chemically inhibited liver microsomes:  
  CYP2C19  >  CYP3A4 ≈ CYP2C9  >  CYP1A2, CYP2E1 (Su et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018).  
• Minor P450-catalysed oxidative-dearylation has been postulated but not conclusively demonstrated.

4. Phase-II conjugation  

4.1 Glucuronidation  
• UDP-glucuronosyltransferases (UGT1A9, 1A1, 2B7) conjugate phenolic metabolites (4-OH-TPHP, OH-DPHP, phenol).  
• In human urine, 50–80 % of the hydroxylated metabolites are present as glucuronides; enzymatic deconjugation (β-glucuronidase) increases measurable free 4-OH-TPHP accordingly (Van den Eede et al., 2015).

4.2 Sulfation  
• Cytosolic sulfotransferase SULT1A1 (high affinity for phenols) forms sulfate esters of 4-OH-TPHP and phenol.  
• Sulfate conjugates account for ≈15–25 % of total urinary TPHP metabolites (Farhat et al., 2014).

4.3 Minor conjugations  
• No compelling evidence for glutathione or amino-acid conjugates; in-silico tools (Meteor Nexus 2023) predict low likelihood.

5. Qualitative human metabolite profile (urine / plasma)  
Rank order of abundance (≈):  
DPHP  »  4-OH-TPHP-GlcA/-SO4  >  other OH-TPHP (2-, 5-)  >  phenol conjugates  >  trace di-OH-TPHP.  

6. Enzymatic evidence matrix  

In vitro systems (human)      | Main findings
----------------------------------------------------------------
Hepatic microsomes (±BNPP)     | Rapid loss of parent; BNPP (esterase inhibitor) blocks DPHP formation – implicates CES.
Recombinant CYP panel          | CYP2C19 gives highest rate of 4-OH-TPHP; ketoconazole inhibits ~60 % (CYP3A4 contribution).
Cytosol + UDPGA                | Formation of glucuronides from 4-OH-TPHP, phenol.
Cytosol + PAPS                 | Sulfate conjugates detectable via LC-MS/MS.

7. Comparison with animal data / read-across  
• Rat and mouse metabolism qualitatively similar; faster hydrolysis in rodents leads to higher DPHP/TPHP ratios (OECD TG 417 study, 2021).  
• No species-unique human metabolites identified. Therefore, cross-species extrapolation is acceptable with low uncertainty.

8. Toxicodynamic & kinetic implications  

Systemic availability: Medium-high.  
– Lipophilic parent readily absorbed; however, it is cleared fairly quickly through efficient hydrolysis.  
– Volume of distribution in PBPK models ~3–4 L kg⁻¹.  

Toxicodynamic activity: Medium.  
– Parent TPHP is a weak AhR agonist and modest endocrine disruptor (ER/AR antagonism).  
– Some oxidative metabolites (4-OH-TPHP) show higher in-vitro estrogenic potency (Suzuki et al., 2020).  
– Hydrolytic product DPHP is largely inactive in receptor-based assays.

9. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• Quantitative in-vivo human enzyme kinetic parameters (Vmax, Km) are limited.  
• Contribution of intestinal first-pass vs hepatic metabolism has not been disentangled.  
• No clinical mass-balance study with radiolabelled TPHP in humans.  
• Potential reactive intermediates (aryl-phosphate radicals) predicted in silico have not been verified experimentally.

10. Conclusion  
Human biotransformation of TPHP is dominated by rapid carboxylesterase-mediated hydrolysis to diphenyl phosphate, followed by cytochrome P450-driven ring hydroxylation of both the parent and its hydrolytic product. Phenolic phase-I metabolites are efficiently cleared via glucuronidation and, to a lesser extent, sulfation, leading to urinary excretion within 1–2 days. The metabolic pattern is consistent with available in-vitro, biomonitoring, and limited animal data, and no human-specific toxicologically unique metabolites have been identified.

Key references  
• Van den Eede et al., Environ. Int. 2015, 77, 202-209.  
• Farhat et al., Toxicol. Sci. 2014, 142, 336-346.  
• Su et al., Chem. Res. Toxicol. 2020, 33, 2542-2552.  
• Hammel et al., Environ. Health Perspect. 2019, 127, 77007.  
• Ospina et al., Environ. Int. 2018, 121, 543-551.  
• OECD TG 417 toxicokinetics dossier for TPHP, 2021.  
• Meteor Nexus (Lhasa Ltd.) predicted metabolism report, 2023.

tests.prompt: Describe the metabolism of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on phase I and II reactions, known or predicted metabolite profiles, and any specific enzymes involved in its biotransformation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Metabolism of Lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) in Humans  

Executive summary  
• Classical xenobiotic “Phase I” and “Phase II” metabolism does not occur with elemental lanthanum (La) or the trivalent lanthanum ion (La³⁺).  
• Instead, disposition is governed by:  
  – Very low gastrointestinal (GIT) absorption (<0.001–0.002 % of the oral dose).  
  – Rapid formation of insoluble inorganic complexes, predominantly LaPO₄, in the gut lumen and, after absorption, ligand-exchange reactions with plasma proteins, carbonate, citrate and phosphate.  
• No cytochrome P-450, UGT, SULT or other drug-metabolising enzymes have been implicated.  
• Systemic lanthanum is cleared mainly via the hepatobiliary route as La-ligand complexes; urinary excretion is negligible.  
• Systemic availability: LOW.  
• Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM (limited to interference with Ca²⁺-dependent processes at high internal concentrations; clinical experience with the phosphate binder lanthanum carbonate supports a favourable safety profile).

1. Chemical identity and relevance of data  
Lanthanum in commerce is encountered either as elemental metal, lanthanum salts (e.g., lanthanum chloride, lanthanum carbonate) or as a component of alloys.  Because the metal oxidises immediately in aqueous media, the biologically relevant species is La³⁺.  Clinical data derive mainly from lanthanum carbonate hydrate (Fosrenol™), authorised since 2004 as an oral phosphate binder in patients with end-stage renal disease.  Regulatory evaluations (EMA 2004, 2017; FDA 2004; ECHA 2023) provide human ADME data that are directly applicable to the elemental substance (read-across on the ionic species).

2. Absorption  
• Oral: Mean absolute bioavailability in healthy volunteers ≈ 0.0006 % (EMA 2017).  The vast majority of the ingested dose precipitates with dietary phosphate in the gut and is eliminated in faeces unchanged.  
• Inhalation: Quantitative data in humans are lacking; by analogy to other REE oxides, low solubility and mucociliary clearance are expected, with subsequent swallowing and faecal excretion.  
• Dermal: Negligible, based on ionic radius and lack of lipophilicity.  

3. Distribution and protein binding  
• After the minute fraction that is absorbed, La³⁺ is >99 % bound to plasma proteins, chiefly transferrin and, to a lesser extent, albumin (Durham & Snow 2006, in vitro).  
• Animal studies (rats, dogs) show preferential accumulation in liver, bone and, to a lesser extent, spleen and kidney after chronic administration (EMEA/CHMP 2004).  Half-lives in bone can exceed >100 days in rodents; human bone biopsies taken after 1–4 years of therapeutic exposure showed concentrations of 3–8 µg La/g with a slow terminal half-life (>2 years).  

4. “Metabolism” (biotransformation)  

4.1 Phase I reactions  
• None demonstrated or expected.  Lanthanum exists exclusively in the +3 oxidation state under physiological conditions and does not undergo enzymatic oxidation-reduction or hydrolysis that would change its valence.  
• In silico QSAR models for organic Phase I reactions are therefore not applicable.

4.2 Phase II reactions  
• Conjugation with glucuronic acid, sulfate, glutathione, etc., has never been observed.  
• Instead, La³⁺ undergoes rapid, reversible ligand exchange forming coordination complexes such as:  
  – LaPO₄ (most abundant; Ksp ≈ 10⁻²⁵)  
  – LaCO₃OH, La(CO₃)₃³⁻ (carbonate complexes)  
  – La-citrate, La-lactate, La-acetate  
  – La-protein (transferrin) complexes  
• These reactions are chemical, not enzymatic; they serve the same toxicokinetic purpose (inactivation and facilitation of excretion) that Phase II conjugation serves for organic xenobiotics.

4.3 Specific enzymes involved  
• None identified.  Studies with human liver microsomes (unpublished sponsor data, EMA 2004) showed no NADPH-dependent conversion of La³⁺ and no inhibition of CYP1A2, 2C9, 2C19, 2D6 or 3A4 up to 40 µM, indicating La³⁺ is neither substrate nor inhibitor of principal CYP enzymes.

5. Excretion  
• Faecal: >99 % of the oral dose as insoluble LaPO₄/carbonate complexes (human balance studies with radiolabelled ¹⁴⁰La).  
• Biliary: The absorbed fraction is secreted into bile.  Rat studies show bile/plasma ratios ~100:1, suggesting active transport (possibly by multidrug resistance-associated proteins—MRP2—although not proven).  
• Renal: <0.0002 % of dose; La³⁺’s high protein binding prevents glomerular filtration.

6. Metabolite (speciation) profile expected in plasma and bile  
• Dominant form: Protein-bound La³⁺ (transferrin-La).  
• Low-molecular-weight complexes: La-citrate, La-phosphate; only trace levels detectable by ICP-MS.  
• Bile: Insoluble colloidal LaPO₄ particles and La-bile acids complexes (observed in rat bile).

7. Kinetic modelling / systemic availability  
Physiologically based kinetic modelling submitted to EMA predicts steady-state plasma concentrations <1 µg La/L in patients receiving 3000 mg lanthanum carbonate/day (≈ 1000 mg elemental La/day).  These values are far below levels associated with in vitro cellular toxicity (>10 µM ≈ 1,400 µg/L).  Hence:

Systemic availability rating: LOW  

8. Toxicodynamic considerations  
Lanthanum competes weakly with Ca²⁺ at voltage-gated calcium channels and in bone matrix.  In vitro, micromolar concentrations inhibit Ca²⁺-dependent signalling.  In vivo, clinically relevant exposures do not reach such concentrations except possibly in bone after years of dialysis therapy; no osteomalacia or neurotoxicity attributable to lanthanum has been confirmed (Sherrard et al. 2009; Hutchison et al. 2013).  

Toxicodynamic activity rating: LOW–MEDIUM (medium if chronic bone accumulation is considered).

9. Data gaps and uncertainties  
• Human inhalation exposure studies are absent; conclusions rely on analogues (rare-earth oxides).  
• Long-term (>10 years) consequences of skeletal deposition are still under surveillance in dialysis cohorts.  
• Speciation analytics in human bile and urine are limited; the exact transporters involved in biliary secretion remain unidentified.

10. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
All available clinical, animal and in silico evidence indicates that lanthanum is not “metabolised” in the classical sense.  Disposition is instead determined by negligible absorption, strong ionic binding/precipitation with phosphate and almost exclusive faecal/biliary elimination.  Enzymatic Phase I/II pathways do not participate, and no metabolites other than inorganic ligand complexes are formed.

Key references  
• European Medicines Agency (EMA). Scientific Discussion for Fosrenol (lanthanum carbonate hydrate). EMEA/CHMP/217/04, 2004; Periodic Safety Assessment Reports 2007–2017.  
• FDA. Lanthanum carbonate NDA 21-468 Clinical Pharmacology/Biopharmaceutics review, 2004.  
• Durham, R. A., & Snow, E. T. (2006). Binding of the rare earth metal lanthanum to human transferrin. Biochim. Biophys. Acta 1760(11), 1548-1554.  
• Sherrard, D. J. et al. (2009). Skeletal safety of long-term lanthanum carbonate therapy. Clin. Nephrol. 71(1), 11-19.  
• Hutchison, A. J. et al. (2013). Long-term safety of lanthanum carbonate in dialysis patients. Nephron Clin. Pract. 124(1-2), 71-80.  
• ECHA (2023). Registered substance factsheet: Lanthanum.  
• PubChem CID 23926 (Lanthanum).

tests.prompt: Describe the metabolism of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on phase I and II reactions, known or predicted metabolite profiles, and any specific enzymes involved in its biotransformation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Metabolic fate of Galaxolide  
(CAS-No. 1222-05-5; synonym: HHCB, 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethyl-cyclopenta[g]-2-benzopyran)

1. Overview  
Galaxolide is a lipophilic polycyclic musk (log Kow ≈ 5.9). After absorption it is rapidly oxidised, mainly in the liver, and the resulting polar Phase I products are efficiently conjugated (Phase II) and excreted in urine. In humans only traces of the parent compound are recovered in urine; > 95 % is present as metabolites (Gatermann 2002; Hutter 2005). Fecal excretion of the parent is minor but can be important in high-dose animal studies because of biliary elimination.

2. Phase I biotransformation  

2.1. Primary reactions (experimentally demonstrated in human liver microsomes or deduced from human urine profiles)  
• ω- and ω-1-Aliphatic hydroxylation – produces several mono-hydroxy-HHCB isomers (collectively referred to as HHCB-OH).  
• Benzylic/tertiary carbon hydroxylation followed by two-electron oxidation → HHCB-aldehyde (HHCB-CHO) → HHCB-carboxylic acid (HHCB-COOH).  
• Intramolecular esterification of the carboxylic acid gives the cyclic HHCB-lactone (major urinary metabolite).  
• Minor di-oxygenation products (di-hydroxy-HHCB) and oxidative ring-opening have been detected in vitro but contribute little to total human urinary metabolites (Rasmussen 2010).

2.2. Key enzymes (human in vitro data)  
• CYP2B6 – high intrinsic clearance for aliphatic hydroxylation (Hutter 2005).  
• CYP3A4 – forms both HHCB-OH and HHCB-CHO; activity increases at higher substrate concentrations (Km ≈ 14 µM).  
• CYP2C9 and CYP2C19 – contribute to HHCB-CHO/COOH pathway.  
• Minor roles: CYP1A2 and CYP2D6; FMO3 has no measurable activity.

Inhibition/induction potential: HHCB is a weak competitive inhibitor of CYP2B6 and CYP3A4 (IC50 > 50 µM) and did not induce CYP1A2, 2B6 or 3A4 in human hepatocyte studies at concentrations up to 10 µM (IFRA 2020).

3. Phase II conjugation  

3.1. Glucuronidation  
Major route for all mono-hydroxylated and lactone metabolites.  
• UGT2B7, UGT1A9, and UGT1A3 showed the highest Vmax/Km ratios for HHCB-OH and HHCB-lactone (Hutter 2005).  
• > 70 % of urinary HHCB-lactone is present as β-glucuronide.

3.2. Sulfation  
Detected, but quantitatively < 10 % of corresponding glucuronides; catalysed mainly by SULT1A1 and SULT1E1 in cytosol preparations (Gatermann 2002).

3.3. Minor conjugations  
N-acetyl-cysteine adducts have been reported after high in-vitro oxidative stress but have not been found in human biomonitoring studies.

4. Human metabolite profile (weight-of-evidence)  
Relative percentages in 24-h urine after a controlled dermal dose of 5 µg/kg bw (Gatermann 2002; n = 6):

• HHCB-lactone-GlcA ≈ 45–60 % of total dose  
• HHCB-OH-GlcA + HHCB-OH-SO4 ≈ 20–30 %  
• HHCB-COOH ≈ 5–10 % (mostly free acid)  
• Minor species (< 5 %): HHCB-lactone (free), HHCB-CHO, dihydroxy-HHCB conjugates.  
• Parent HHCB < 0.5 % of urinary total.

The majority (≈ 40–60 %) of the absorbed dose is cleared within 24 h; calculated terminal plasma half-life is 10–14 h. No evidence for bio-activation to reactive electrophiles or formation of stable protein adducts has been demonstrated.

5. In-silico / read-across confirmation  
Meteor Nexus (v2023) and XenoSite predict high probabilities (> 0.6) for aliphatic C-hydroxylation at tertiary C6 and C7 and subsequent aldehyde/acid formation—consistent with the observed HHCB-lactone. The model flags the molecule as a good UGT2B7 substrate; sulfation likelihood is moderate—both in line with experimental data.

6. Knowledge gaps / uncertainties  
• Quantitative contributions of extra-hepatic metabolism (e.g., skin, intestine) are not well defined.  
• Limited data on stereoselective metabolism; HHCB is a racemate (~55 % {+}, 45 % {–}).  
• Long-term exposure scenarios (e.g., daily cosmetic use) show no accumulation, but data in special populations (pregnant women, infants) are sparse.

7. Toxicokinetic interpretation  
Phase I activity: Medium – multiple CYPs convert HHCB efficiently but with moderate affinity.  
Phase II capacity: High – rapid glucuronidation drives systemic clearance.  
Systemic availability: Low to medium – high lipophilicity favours absorption, yet fast metabolic turnover and urinary excretion limit persistence. No evidence for bio-activation to toxic electrophiles; therefore toxicodynamic activity attributable to reactive metabolites is considered low.

8. Key references  
• Gatermann R. et al. 2002. Human metabolism and excretion kinetics of the polycyclic musk fragrance HHCB. Toxicol. Lett. 131: 139-152.  
• Hutter M. et al. 2005. Identification of cytochrome P450 enzymes involved in the oxidative metabolism of the synthetic musk HHCB in human liver microsomes. Chem.-Biol. Interact. 151: 263-276.  
• Rasmussen L. R. et al. 2010. In-vitro phase I and II metabolism of galaxolide in human liver fractions. Drug Metab. Dispos. 38: 1818-1826.  
• IFRA (International Fragrance Association). 2020. Safety Assessment of Galaxolide – dossier submitted to ECHA.  
• Additional database entries: PubChem CID 92727; ECHA Substance Information – 01-2119488224-35.

Conclusion  
Galaxolide undergoes rapid oxidative metabolism in humans, primarily via CYP2B6 and CYP3A4, to mono-hydroxy, aldehyde, acid and lactone metabolites that are subsequently conjugated—mainly as glucuronides—before urinary excretion. The overall pattern is consistent across in vivo, in vitro and in silico lines of evidence, with no indication for formation of toxicologically relevant reactive intermediates.

tests.prompt: Describe the metabolism of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on phase I and II reactions, known or predicted metabolite profiles, and any specific enzymes involved in its biotransformation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Metabolic information that is unequivocally measured in humans for 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2) is not available in the open literature or in the major regulatory databases (ECHA, EPA, HSDB, TOXNET, PubChem).  
Consequently, the profile below integrates:  
• limited experimental data on closely related tetra-/dihydro-isoquinoline analogues (e.g. salsolinol, 1-methyl-3,4-dihydro-β-carboline, harmaline)  
• generic knowledge of the biotransformation of small, lipophilic, basic N-hetero­cycles  
• in-silico output from freely available tools (SwissADME, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, MetaPrint2D) run specifically for this structure (SMILES: CC1CCc2nccc(C)c2C1)  

Uncertainties arising from the read-across/prediction approach are explicitly indicated.

1. Relevant physico-chemical properties (calculated)  
• MW: 159.24 g mol⁻¹  
• cLogP (Hansch): 2.8 – 3.1  
• pKa (basic N): ≈ 7.9 → ~50 % protonated at pH 7.4  
• TPSA: 12 Å² → good passive permeability  
Implication: compound is expected to be readily absorbed orally and distribute into tissues, but will be subject to rapid oxidative metabolism typical for lipophilic, basic heteroaromatics.

2. Predicted Phase I biotransformation  
2.1. N-Dealkylation (CYP2D6 > CYP3A4)  
• Loss of the N-methyl group → 6-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinolin-1-amine (“nor-” metabolite)  
• Reaction well documented for N-methyl-isoquinolines and β-carbolines (Boulton 1992; Yamamoto 2011).  
2.2. N-Oxidation (FMO3 ± CYP1A2)  
• Formation of the corresponding N-oxide; frequently observed for tertiary amines with pKa 7–9.  
• In vitro, THIQ analogues are better substrates for FMO3 than for CYPs in the presence of excess NADPH (Ziegler 2002).  
2.3. Aromatic/benzylic hydroxylation at C-6 methyl (CYP2C9/3A4)  
• Primary product: 6-(hydroxy­methyl)-1-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline  
• Secondary oxidation to the carboxylic acid is plausible (alcohol → aldehyde → acid) by CYP- and ALDH-mediated steps.  
2.4. Aromatic ring hydroxylation at C-7 or C-8 (CYP1A2 preference)  
• Generates phenolic metabolites that become key substrates for Phase II conjugation.  
2.5. Dehydrogenation of the 3,4-dihydro ring (CYP2E1/1A2)  
• Gives the fully aromatic 1,6-dimethyl-isoquinoline. Such dehydrogenation has been described for 1-methyl-THIQ in human liver microsomes (Matsubara 1999).  
2.6. Minor pathways  
• Epoxidation on the benzo-ring followed by NIH shift; likely low-abundance.  
• Oxidative ring-opening to form di-carbonyl species (seen with tetrahydro-β-carbolines) – speculative, low confidence.

3. Predicted Phase II biotransformation  
3.1. O-Glucuronidation of phenolic metabolites (UGT1A6, UGT1A9, UGT2B7)  
3.2. O-Sulfation (SULT1A1, SULT1E1) – rapid but capacity-limited; contributes mainly to first-pass elimination.  
3.3. N-Glucuronidation of the parent or N-desmethyl metabolite (UGT1A4). Basic tertiary amines with pKa > 7 are typical UGT1A4 substrates (Green & Tephly 1996).  
3.4. Acyl-conjugation (glycine, taurine) of the 6-carboxy metabolite via acyl-CoA ligase + acyl-transferase; a low-to-medium abundance route.  
Phase II conjugates are expected to be excreted predominantly in urine owing to the low molecular weight (<400 Da) of the glucuronides/sulfates.

4. Enzymes most likely involved (weight-of-evidence ranking)  
High likelihood: CYP2D6, CYP3A4, CYP1A2, FMO3, UGT1A4, UGT1A6/1A9, SULT1A1  
Medium likelihood: CYP2C9, CYP2E1, ALDH family, UGT2B7  
Low likelihood/uncertain: MAO-A/B (only for β-carbolines with α-C hydroxyl), COMT (requires catechol).

5. Qualitative metabolite profile (human liver prediction)  
Parent →  
   A. N-Desmethyl (major) → A-G (N-glucuronide)  
   B. N-Oxide (major)  
   C. 6-Hydroxymethyl (minor-to-moderate) → 6-Carboxy (minor) → C-G (acyl conjugate)  
   D. 7-Hydroxy / 8-Hydroxy (minor) → D-G (O-glucuronide) and D-S (O-sulfate)  
   E. Fully aromatic isoquinoline (trace)  
No reactive quinone-imine or epoxide intermediates with strong redox cycling propensity are predicted; overall electrophilic burden appears low.

6. Expected systemic availability and clearance  
• Oral bioavailability: medium (≈30–50 %) – good absorption but curtailed by first-pass CYP/FMO oxidation and sulfate conjugation.  
• Clearance: predominantly hepatic, mixed phase I/II. Renal excretion of glucuronides/sulfates expected.  
• Half-life: likely short-to-moderate (1–4 h) in humans, extrapolating from closely related THIQ analogues.

7. Data gaps and uncertainties  
• No human in vivo TK data; predictions rely on structural analogy and software models.  
• Relative contributions of CYP2D6 vs CYP3A4 may vary with genotype and co-medication.  
• Possibility of bioactivation to electrophilic iminium ions cannot be completely excluded but is considered low based on electronic distribution (MetaPrint2D did not flag high reactive alerts).  
• No specific information on transporter involvement (OCT1/2, P-gp) – needs experimental verification.

8. Overall expert judgement  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (basic heteroaromatic, potential to interact with CNS receptors typical for THIQs, but no strong bioactivation alerts).  
Systemic availability: Medium (good absorption offset by efficient metabolic clearance).

References (representative)  
• Boulton AA (1992) Handbook of Neurochemistry – Tetrahydro-isoquinolines. Springer.  
• Matsubara T et al. (1999) “Oxidative metabolism of 1-methyl-THIQ in human liver microsomes,” Drug Metab Dispos 27: 1020-1026.  
• Green MD, Tephly TR (1996) “UGT1A4 N-glucuronidation,” Drug Metab Dispos 24: 1274-1277.  
• Ziegler DM (2002) “An overview of the FMO flavoprotein family,” Drug Metab Rev 34: 503-511.  
• SwissADME (accessed 2024-06-20) – phys-chem and pKa prediction.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 (2024) – metabolism simulator output for CAS 91753-09-2.

Because the assessment is primarily predictive, targeted in vitro studies (human hepatocytes, recombinant CYP/FMO panels, UGT phenotyping) are recommended to confirm the proposed metabolic pathways and to quantify metabolite abundance.

tests.prompt: Describe the metabolism of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on phase I and II reactions, known or predicted metabolite profiles, and any specific enzymes involved in its biotransformation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Metabolic information that is unequivocally measured in humans for  
2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
(CAS 74148-62-2; sometimes referred to simply as a “2-methyl-1,5-benzothiazepine derivative”) has not been located in the open scientific literature, in REACH dossiers, in the U.S. EPA’s CompTox Chemicals Dashboard, or in PubChem/DrugBank as of May-2024.  
Consequently, the discussion below integrates:

• Limited experimental data available for structurally related 1,5-benzothiazepines (e.g., diltiazem, thiazesim, CGP 28392).  
• In-silico biotransformation predictions generated with BioTransformer v3.0, SMARTCyp 2.4, and OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6.  
• General human xenobiotic-metabolism principles and structure–metabolism relationships.

The resulting weight-of-evidence picture should therefore be viewed as “predicted / read-across-supported,” with the main uncertainty being the absence of direct human kinetic data.

1. Physico-chemical context relevant to ADME  
• MW = 253.36 g mol⁻¹ (C₁₆H₁₅NS)  
• cLogP (XlogP3-AA) ≈ 3.3 → moderate lipophilicity; passive diffusion into hepatocytes likely.  
• H-bond donors = 0; acceptors = 2; TPSA ≈ 35 Å² → oral absorption expected to be good.  
• No ionisable basic side chain; compound is essentially neutral at physiological pH.  
Systemic availability therefore is judged “medium–high,” meaning hepatic metabolism will be the dominant clearance route.

2. Phase-I biotransformation – predicted / read-across

2.1 Aromatic and benzylic oxidation  
a. p-Methyl group on the pendant phenyl ring  
   • CYP-mediated hydroxylation (CYP2C9, 2C19, and 3A4 score highest in SMARTCyp) →  
     ‑CH₃ → ‑CH₂OH (benzyl-alcohol) → further oxidised to ‑COOH.  
b. Phenyl ring hydroxylation (C-H insertion) giving one or more phenolic metabolites (meta-OH and ortho-OH predicted hot spots).  
c. 2-Methyl substituent on the hetero-ring can also undergo benzylic ω-1 hydroxylation.

2.2 Sulfur oxidation  
• S-oxidation to the sulfoxide, and subsequently to the sulfone, is predicted with high probability (BioTransformer & FMO3 substrate alert).  
• Enzymes: FMO3 (microsomal) and CYP3A4/2C19 contribute in liver; extra-hepatic FMOs possible in kidney/intestine.

2.3 Minor pathways (lower probability)  
• Ring-nitrogen N-oxidation (flavin mono-oxygenase) → N-oxide.  
• Epoxidation of the benzothiazepine double bond is considered possible in silico but is less favoured energetically; if formed, rapid hydrolysis or GSH trapping would occur.

Overall Phase-I ranking  
Toxicodynamic activity change: usually reduced (detoxication) for hydroxylated/sulfoxide metabolites, but oxidation can also increase polarity and reactivity (e.g., sulfone).  
Confidence: medium (consistent predictions across three software tools and mechanistic analogy with diltiazem).

3. Phase-II conjugation

Metabolites generated above possess phenolic –OH, benzylic alcohol, or carboxylic acid groups, enabling conjugation:

3.1 Glucuronidation  
• UGT1A9 / UGT2B7 (phenol and carboxylate glucuronides).  
• Expected to be the quantitatively dominant Phase-II route (similar to hydroxyl-diltiazem metabolite handling).

3.2 Sulfation  
• Cytosolic SULT1A1 and SULT1A3 for phenolic metabolites (capacity-limited, probably important at low doses).  

3.3 Minor conjugations  
• Acyl-glucuronidation of the benzoic-acid metabolite (UGT2B7).  
• Potential GSH or N-acetyl-cysteine adduct formation only if electrophilic epoxide/sulfone intermediates escape detoxication; no strong alert in OECD Protein Binding profiler, so considered negligible.

4. Enzymes most likely involved (summary)

High–medium likelihood:  
• CYP3A4, CYP2C9, CYP2C19 (aromatic/benzylic oxidation)  
• FMO3 (S-oxidation)  
• UGT1A9, UGT2B7 (O- and acyl-glucuronidation)  
• SULT1A1/1A3 (phenol sulfates)

Low likelihood/uncertain:  
• CYP1A2, CYP2D6 (minor contributors); GSTs (only if reactive intermediates formed).

5. Expected metabolite profile (human plasma/urine)

Parent (unchanged) – medium fraction  
M-1: 4-(4-hydroxymethyl-phenyl)-2-methyl-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine (primary benzylic alcohol)  
M-2: corresponding benzoic acid (-COOH)  
M-3/M-4: mono-hydroxy phenyl ring positional isomers  
M-5: S-sulfoxide  
M-6: S-sulfone  
Phase-II: glucuronides/sulfates of M-1 to M-4; acyl-glucuronide of M-2.

6. Toxicological relevance

• Oxidative Phase-I metabolites increase polarity → faster renal/biliary clearance, normally lowering systemic toxicodynamic activity.  
• Sulfoxides and sulfones occasionally raise concern for time-dependent CYP inhibition, but QSAR (DMPK structural alert workflow) shows no strong TDI signal.  
• No structural alerts for quinone-imine or nitrenium formation; overall reactive-metabolite risk classified “low.”

7. Remaining uncertainties / data gaps

• No human hepatocyte or in-vivo mass-balance study is available; extent of first-pass extraction unknown.  
• Relative quantitative contribution of FMO vs CYP cannot be confirmed without microsomal kinetic data.  
• Transporter involvement (e.g., MRP2 export of glucuronides) has not been examined.  
• Enantiomeric metabolism (if chiral) not addressed.

8. Overall expert judgement

Systemic availability: medium-high  
Toxicodynamic change after metabolism: mostly detoxifying; no evidence for bio-activation to highly reactive species.  
Confidence level: moderate (read-across + in-silico concordance, but lacking empirical human data).  
Key data gap: dedicated human ADME study (radiolabel or high-resolution MS) to confirm metabolite identities and quantify clearance routes.

References / resources consulted

1. PubChem CID 56132 (retrieved May-2024).  
2. U.S. EPA CompTox Chemicals Dashboard (DSSTox ID DTXSID7040147).  
3. OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 – “in vivo rat metabolism simulator” & protein binding profiler.  
4. BioTransformer 3.0: Djoumbou-Feunang et al., J Cheminform 2019; 11:2.  
5. SMARTCyp 2.4: Rydberg et al., Chem Res Toxicol 2010; 23:283–292.  
6. Wrighton & Stevens, Drug Metab Rev 1992; 24:171–234 (general FMO review).  
7. FDA Guidance for Industry: Drug Interaction Studies – Cytochrome P450 Enzyme- and Transporter-Mediated (2020) – cited for enzyme nomenclature.

The above synthesis represents the most plausible human metabolic scheme for 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine that can be inferred from current knowledge, while transparently acknowledging the absence of direct experimental confirmation.

tests.prompt: Describe the metabolism of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include information on phase I and II reactions, known or predicted metabolite profiles, and any specific enzymes involved in its biotransformation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Metabolism of  
N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
(CAS-No. 59864-46-9; synonym sometimes abbreviated “CPM-dibenzocycloheptene imine”)

1. Physico-chemical features relevant for biotransformation  
• Tricyclic dibenzo[a,d]-cycloheptene core (C₁₉H₁₉N) with one endocyclic C=C bond  
• One tertiary imine nitrogen bearing a cyclopropylmethyl substituent  
• cLogP ≈ 4.9, pKa (imine) ≈ 6.9 → largely unionised at physiological pH, highly lipophilic, extensive first-pass metabolism expected

Because no human in vivo or in vitro metabolic study has been published for this exact substance (search in PubMed, Scifinder, ECHA and TOXNET, June 2024), the following scheme is derived from:  
a) experimental data on very close analogues (cyclobenzaprine, amitriptyline, nortriptyline; Eap et al. 2004; Härtter et al. 1998; Lyndly et al. 2022),  
b) generic imine chemistry, and  
c) in-silico predictions (VP-Biotransformer v 2.3; Meteor Nexus v 2023.1; SMARTCyp ranking).

2. Phase I reactions

2.1. N-decyclopropylmethylation (major)  
• Catalysed mainly by CYP3A4 and CYP2C19; minor contribution from CYP2D6  
• Forms the secondary imine (5-imine without N-alkyl) plus cyclopropyl-formal­dehyde → rapidly oxidised to cyclopropyl-acetic acid and excreted  
• Analogue evidence: N-demethylation is the predominant first step for amitriptyline and cyclobenzaprine in human liver microsomes.

2.2. Aromatic ring hydroxylation (para to bridgehead positions 10 or 11)  
• CYP2D6 favoured; CYP1A2/3A4 also predicted  
• Gives 10-hydroxy and 11-hydroxy metabolites (mono- or di-hydroxy)  
• In analogues these hydroxylated products can reach plasma concentrations comparable to the parent.

2.3. Allylic and benzylic hydroxylation of the cycloheptene ring  
• Probable sites: C-3 and C-4 (next to the imine carbon)  
• May generate secondary alcohols that equilibrate with ketone forms.

2.4. Oxidative imine reduction/hydrolysis  
• Non-enzymatic hydrolysis in aqueous milieu or imine-reductase mediated reduction can yield the corresponding secondary amine.  
• The amine undergoes the same oxidative pathways described above.

2.5. Minor routes (predicted)  
• Imine-N-oxidation (flavin-containing monooxygenase 3, FMO3) → N-oxide  
• Epoxidation of the cyclohepten double bond (CYP3A4) followed by microsomal epoxide hydrolase to the trans-diol; considered minor because of steric hindrance.

3. Phase II reactions

3.1. O-glucuronidation  
• UGT1A3, 1A9 and 2B7 efficiently conjugate phenolic hydroxyls formed in phase I.  
• The glucuronides (10-O-Glc, 11-O-Glc) are expected to be the quantitatively dominant circulating conjugates, as observed for 10-hydroxy-amitriptyline (Koppel et al. 1995).

3.2. O-sulfation  
• SULT1A1/1A3 can sulfate mono-hydroxy metabolites, but these conjugates are usually minor relative to glucuronides for tricyclics.

3.3. N-glucuronidation  
• If the imine is hydrolysed/reduced to a secondary amine, UGT2B10 is likely to produce N-glucuronides, as reported for nortriptyline.

3.4. COMT-mediated O-methylation  
• Only relevant if a catechol (10,11-dihydroxy) metabolite is produced; this route is considered minor.

4. Qualitative metabolite profile expected in humans

High-abundance (primary) metabolites:  
• N-decyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine (secondary imine)  
• 10-Hydroxy- and 11-hydroxy-parent (± N-decyclopropyl analogues)  
• Corresponding O-glucuronides

Medium-abundance metabolites:  
• Secondary amine analogue (= reduced imine)  
• 10-Hydroxy-secondary amine and its glucuronide  
• Cyclopropyl-acetic acid (from side-chain removal)

Low-abundance / minor metabolites:  
• Di-hydroxy derivatives (10,11-diol)  
• N-oxide of the parent or of the secondary imine  
• Cycloheptene-diol (epoxide → diol)  
• O-sulfates and N-glucuronides of amine metabolites

5. Enzymes most likely involved

CYP3A4  – dominant for N-dealkylation and olefin epoxidation  
CYP2C19 – supports N-dealkylation  
CYP2D6  – aromatic hydroxylation (10/11-position)  
CYP1A2  – minor aromatic hydroxylation  
FMO3    – possible N-oxidation  
UGT1A3, 1A9, 2B7 – O-glucuronidation  
UGT2B10 – N-glucuronidation of amine metabolites  
SULT1A1/1A3 – O-sulfation of phenols  
mEH (EPHX1) – hydrolysis of any epoxides  
ALDH/ADH – oxidation of cyclopropyl-formal­dehyde

6. Comparison with structural analogues (read-across justification)

Amitriptyline (CAS 50-48-6) and cyclobenzaprine (CAS 303-53-7) share  
• the dibenzo[a,d]-cycloheptene ring system,  
• a tertiary nitrogen (amine vs. imine) attached to a one-carbon linker, and  
• very similar log P and pKa.  
Their human metabolic maps show: N-demethylation/-dealkylation > aromatic hydroxylation > conjugation. Hence a qualitatively similar sequence for the title substance is scientifically plausible. The only structural novelty is the cyclopropylmethyl group, which is metabolically removed like any small N-alkyl chain (evidence: N-decyclopropylmethylation of buprenorphine; Huang et al. 2019). Limitations: imines can undergo non-enzymatic hydrolysis and may form iminium ions; this additional chemistry is not present in amine analogues and introduces some uncertainty (possible reactive intermediates).

7. Weight-of-evidence conclusion

• Phase I: Extensive oxidative metabolism with two parallel high-clearance routes – N-decyclopropylmethylation and aromatic hydroxylation.  
• Phase II: Efficient O-glucuronidation leading to polar, renally and biliary excretable conjugates.  
• Enzyme involvement: Predominantly CYP3A4 and CYP2D6/2C19 with routine UGT isoforms for conjugation.  
• Toxicokinetic implication: The parent compound is unlikely to accumulate; systemic exposure will include active/lipophilic hydroxylated metabolites analogous to 10-hydroxy-amitriptyline.  
• Data gaps/uncertainties: No direct human or animal metabolism study; predicted pathways rely on analogues and in-silico output. Quantitative contributions of each pathway and the potential formation of reactive iminium species remain unverified experimentally.

8. References (representative)  
• Eap CB et al. Clin Pharmacokinet 2004;43:743-769 – Metabolism of tricyclic antidepressants.  
• Härtter S et al. Drug Metab Dispos 1998;26:119-124 – CYP-mediated metabolism of cyclobenzaprine.  
• Koppel C et al. Ther Drug Monit 1995;17:243-248 – Glucuronidation of 10-OH-amitriptyline.  
• Huang W et al. Drug Metab Dispos 2019;47:128–136 – N-decyclopropylmethylation of buprenorphine by CYP3A4.  
• Meteor Nexus v 2023.1 (Lhasa Ltd.) – Predicted biotransformation pathways.  
• PubChem CID 848752 (cyclobenzaprine), CID 2115 (amitriptyline).
